title: The Algorithm and Me

modules.title: Who Picked What You're Watching?
modules.sub: Trace a single video recommendation back to its source.
modules.description: Learners follow the story of a character named Sam who can't stop watching one type of video and has no idea why. Through a visual trail-tracing activity, learners map the data signals — watch time, clicks, skips, time of day — that fed the recommendation engine. The goal is a personal 'aha' moment: these are my choices, and they're being read.

modules.title: Training on You
modules.sub: Discover that your behaviour teaches the algorithm daily.
modules.description: Learners act as both the user and the algorithm in a turn-based story game. When they play the user, they make normal daily app choices. When they play the algorithm, they use those same choices to predict what the user wants next. Switching roles is the core learning device — it makes the data relationship feel real and two-directional, not abstract.

modules.title: The Feed That Fits Too Well
modules.sub: Explore what happens when AI only shows you what you like.
modules.description: Sam's feed is now perfectly tuned — but Sam is bored, a little anxious, and only hears one kind of opinion. Learners decide: should Sam reset the algorithm, diversify their watch habits, or leave the platform? Each choice has a story consequence, and the module ends with learners writing one thing they might want to do differently on their own apps.

modules.title: Take Back Your Feed
modules.sub: Design your own rules for an algorithm you'd actually trust.
modules.description: Learners design their ideal recommendation system — choosing what signals it should use, what it should never do, and what content categories it should balance. They produce a simple 'Algorithm Rules Card': a set of five rules written in plain language that a fair algorithm would follow. This card is the course exit artifact and a tool they can apply to their own digital life.

synopsis: A curious, story-led exploration of the invisible systems that choose what you see, hear, and read every day — and how you can start to notice them. Perfect for young learners ready to ask: wait, who decided this for me?
rationale: Recommendation algorithms shape children's information environment more powerfully than almost any other AI system, yet there is almost no age-appropriate education that helps young learners understand or push back on them.
learning_outcome: Students can explain in simple terms how a recommendation algorithm uses their behaviour as training data, identify at least one way algorithmic feeds can limit their perspective, and articulate personal rules for more intentional use of algorithmic platforms.
source_signals: HUMANS VS AI 🤖 | What Is AI ? | Artificial Intelligence Explained | Kids Learning Videos | EMoMee
